symptoms.symptom_name: Unilateral throbbing headache preceded by visual aura, evolving over 48 hours

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Unilateral throbbing pain with preceding visual aura is a hallmark migraine phenotype.
Tension headache is typically bilateral, pressing/tightening and non-throbbing, without aura.
Photophobia and phonophobia are core migraine associated features.
Photophobia occurs in meningitis too, so alone it does not discriminate — must be interpreted with fever/meningism.

symptoms.symptom_name: Persistent vomiting with inability to retain oral fluids or medication

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Vomiting is a common severe-migraine feature and, with gastric stasis, defeats oral absorption — driving the need for a parenteral route.

symptoms.symptom_name: Visual aura (flickering/zigzag lights) preceding the headache

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A stereotyped visual aura preceding the headache is characteristic of migraine with aura.
Aura does not occur in tension-type headache.

symptoms.symptom_name: Gradual crescendo onset over hours (absence of thunderclap onset)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of thunderclap (maximal-within-seconds) onset argues strongly against SAH; gradual build-up is typical of migraine.
Gradual crescendo build-up is the usual migraine onset pattern.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever, no neck stiffness, no rash, clear sensorium (absence of meningism)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Afebrile with no neck stiffness, rash or altered sensorium argues strongly against bacterial meningitis.

symptoms.symptom_name: No focal neurological deficit on gross screen (normal power, speech, facial symmetry)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of focal deficit reduces the likelihood of stroke/SAH, though a full neurological exam is clinician-only.

symptoms.symptom_name: Failed oral triptan rescue (sumatriptan 50mg vomited ~24h ago)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Not diagnostic, but establishes that oral rescue has failed and a parenteral route is required to abort the attack.

symptoms.symptom_name: Long-standing migraine history with increasing attack frequency

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A prior stereotyped migraine history raises the prior probability that this attack is another (severe) migraine — but does not by itself exclude a new secondary cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (pulse ~105) attributed to pain and volume depletion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Sinus tachycardia is non-specific — here consistent with pain and volume depletion rather than an independent pathology; exact rate is a clinician/device measurement.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Unilateral throbbing pain + preceding aura + photophobia/phonophobia + vomiting together form a high-probability migraine constellation in a known migraineur, especially once the dangerous mimics are screened negative.

The absence of thunderclap onset, meningism and focal deficit together clears the important-not-to-miss secondary causes, allowing convergence on migraine flare without emergency-ambulance escalation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated in this telehealth encounter — the red-flag screen for SAH/secondary pathology is negative; urgent neuroimaging is reserved for a positive red flag (thunderclap onset, focal deficit, papilloedema, change in usual headache character).
Not indicated — no clinical features of meningitis or SAH; would only follow neuroimaging if a red flag emerged.
Reasonable at in-person review to assess hydration/electrolytes given vomiting, and as part of preventive-therapy work-up; not an acute telehealth-orderable requirement.
